title: Methods and devices for delivering therapeutic materials to the intervertebral disc
abstract: A method of delivering a therapeutic agent to a nucleus pulposus of an intervertebral disc is provided. The method comprises inserting a delivery tool containing the therapeutic agent through an anterior portion, a lateral portion, or an anterolateral portion of an annulus fibrosus and into the nucleus pulposus of the intervertebral disc; and delivering the therapeutic agent to the nucleus pulposus of the intervertebral disc. Devices and kits are also provided.
claims: Claims WHAT IS CLAIMED IS: 1. A method of delivering a therapeutic agent to a nucleus pulposus of an intervertebral disc, the method comprising inserting a delivery tool containing the therapeutic agent through an anterior portion, a lateral portion, or an anterolateral portion of an annulus fibrosus and into the nucleus pulposus of the intervertebral disc; and delivering the therapeutic agent to the nucleus pulposus of the intervertebral disc. 2. The method of claim 1, wherein the method comprises forming an entry point of delivery with a surgical tool between an endplate and an annular body before inserting the delivery tool, and inserting the delivery tool through the anterior portion or the lateral portion. 3. The method of claim 1, wherein the method comprises forming a posterior entry point of delivery with a surgical tool, and inserting the delivery tool is done from a lateral or anterolateral portion. 4. The method of claim 2, wherein the surgical tool is a drill. 5. A device for delivering a therapeutic agent to a nucleus pulposus of an intervertebral disc, the device comprising a cannula configured to slidably receive the therapeutic agent and a needle, the cannula having a distal end, the distal end configured to penetrate through an anterior portion, a lateral portion, a posterior portion or an anterolateral portion of an annulus fibrosus and into the nucleus pulposus of the intervertebral disc, the needle configured to slide in at least a portion of the cannula, the needle having a tip configured to contact the nucleus pulposus of the intervertebral disc and deliver the therapeutic agent from the cannula to the nucleus pulposus of the intervertebral disc. 6. The device of claim 5, wherein (i) the needle is slidable in a retracted position where the tip of the needle is within the cannula and the needle is slidable in an extended position where the tip of the needle protrudes from the distal end of the cannula; or (ii) the device further comprises a probe disposed about at least a portion of the needle, the probe having an end configured to separate a portion of the annulus fibrosus from an endplate of the intervertebral disc. 7. The device of claim 5, wherein (i) the distal end of the cannula comprises a sharp beveled tip that is sharper relative to the tip of the needle; or (ii) the needle is more flexible than the cannula so as to provide tactile feedback to a user. 8. The device of claim 5, wherein the device comprises a resilient member configured to extend the needle. 9. The device of claim 5, wherein the therapeutic agent comprises a depot, a gel, hydrogel, stem cells, discogenic cells, platelet rich plasma, blood, bone marrow concentrate, cytokines, growth and differentiation factors, buffers, saline, hyaluronic acid, micronized autologous intervertebral disc, micronized allogenic intervertebral disc, cartilage, or a combination thereof. 10. The device of claim 5, wherein the cannula and/or the needle is curved. 11. The device of claim 5, wherein the needle is coaxial to the cannula and the needle comprises a shape memory material. 12. The device of claim 11, wherein the shape memory material comprises nitinol. 13. A method of administering a therapeutic agent to an intervertebral disc, the method comprising inserting a catheter into a blood vessel that leads to an annulus fibrosus or a nucleus pulposus of the intervertebral disc; and delivering the therapeutic agent through the catheter at or near the nucleus pulposus of the intervertebral disc. 14. The method of claim 13, wherein (i) the blood vessel is a segmental vein that leads to a basivertebral vein; (ii) the blood vessel is a segmental vein that leads to a basivertebral artery; or (iii) the blood vessel has direct access to the annulus fibrosus or the nucleus pulposus of the intervertebral disc; or (iv) the blood vessel has indirect access to the annulus fibrosus or the nucleus pulposus of the intervertebral disc. 15. The method of claim 14, wherein the catheter is guided axially to penetrate a portion of the cancellous bone of the vertebral body and the portion of an endplate before delivering of the therapeutic agent. 16. The method of claim 15, wherein a void is formed when a portion of the cancellous bone of the vertebral body and a portion of the endplate is penetrated. 17. The method of claim 16, wherein the void has direct access to the nucleus pulposus, and the therapeutic agent is delivered into the void by a drug depot for sustained release of the therapeutic agent to the nucleus pulposus over a period of time. 18. The method of claim 16, wherein a curved instrument is inserted into the void, an annulus fibrosus of the intervertebral disc and the nucleus pulposus is accessed by the curved instrument, and the therapeutic agent is delivered to the annulus fibrosus by a drug depot for sustained release of the therapeutic agent to the nucleus pulposus over a period of time. 19. The method of claim 17, wherein the drug depot comprises a hydrogel having a selected release profile. 20. The method of claim 13, wherein the therapeutic agent comprises a depot, a gel, hydrogel, stem cells, discogenic cells, platelet rich plasma, blood, bone marrow concentrate, cytokines, growth and differentiation factors, buffers, saline, hyaluronic acid, micronized autologous intervertebral disc, micronized allogenic intervertebral disc, cartilage, or a combination thereof.
